sections.The card: A jester holding a sword sits in a chair in front of a curtain, looking back over his shoulder. The jester wears mainly red. He has a three-pointed hat with bells and a short cloak over a tight-fitting outfit. His red legging are buttoned over his pointy blue shoes. He holds a red sword up in his right hand, and has a white recorder in his left hand. To his right, there is a green table with a letter and a quill pen in an inkwell. An orange and purple lute rests against the left side of his chair. A magpie perches on the back of the chair over the jester's left shoulder.
sections.Interpretation: The King of Swords is based on Stańczyk, the celebrated medieval Polish hero. This depiction in particular is from Jan Matejko's painting.

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: A clown stands in the desert, roping a gigantic coin in a lasso over his head. The clown wears a blue wig and a light-blue ten-gallon hat with a red five-pointed star on it. He also has a white and green button-down shirt and gloves, a bolo tie, wide blue pants with fringe down the sides, a red belt, and red pointy-toed shoes. He holds the coiled end of the rope at his waist with his right hand. His left hand is high in the air grasping the long winding rope that loops around the large coin. Around the clown there is a desert landscape of yellow mountains and green and blue cacti with red and yellow blossoms. The yellow coin is up in the sky, dominating the top half of the card. In the center of the coin there is a flower with four round blue petals, and four red leaf spikes between the petals. The middle of the flower is red with twelve black dots. The flower is contained in a round shape, resembling another flower with pointed petals or a stylized sun. Around this circle, there are sixteen green and gold triangles and sixteen red triangles.
sections.Interpretation: In the typical Marseille tarot, the Ace of Coins appears to be lying on the ground, among vines and roots. This version is the opposite, with the clown pulling the coin down from the sky. The lasso is a very literal take on "harnessing potential."

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: In the foreground, a clown smiles as he sweeps up a coin and two banana peels with a wide red broom. In the background, another clown loses his grip on a coin as he slips on a banana peel and falls backward, looking surprised. The two clowns are in the same uniform. They both have red noses, orange and white checkered shirts, blue overalls, red and green boots, red and green visored hats, and red and green gloves. The clown in the background also has a yellow star on the bib of his overalls. The coins are orange, decorated with five-petaled flowers with pale blue centers. The ground is sandy and there is a fence in the background.
sections.Interpretation: One traditional reading of the Two of Coins is taking time to rest and restore, so I used the image of janitors sweeping up between the acts at the rodeo. (These clowns were inspired by the janitors at the Bread and Puppet Theater.) In other readings, such as the Etteilla system, this card relates to a mix-up or embarrassment, as illustrated by the poor gentleman in the back. Will you clean up, or slip on the great comic banana-peel of life?

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: The bones of a clown in the desert. The smiling skull has a round red nose and coins in its eye sockets. It wears a blue cowboy hat with a red band. A hand with a red glove sticks out of the ground holding a third coin. Each of the three coins is black, with a yellow ring around the outside, and a yellow flower enclosing a four-petaled red flower in the middle. A red scorpion crawls by the jawbone, and the blue tail of a snake is visible on the right side of the card next to the bony arm. In the foreground rests a white bone with six holes drilled in it. The ground is yellow and sandy, with various small green plants.
sections.Interpretation: Small plants and animals spring up around the grinning skull of a clown lost in the desert (and the bone flute from Arcanum XIII). One skeletal hand reaches out of the ground to entice the explorer with a coin. This card is about new growth, and the constancy of the earth above all – the desert swallows clowns and gives up gold.

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: A mustached clown in a white suit and ten-gallon hat stands with his hands on his hips in the middle of four oil rigs in a desert landscape. The clown wears a red shirt and pale blue tie, which matches his gloves and cowboy boots. He also has a gold belt buckle, and a dollar sign is drawn in the middle of his hat. The oil rigs are red and blue, and each has a yellow and black coin on it, decorated with an orange four-petaled flower.
sections.Interpretation: A good old Texas oil-clown stands content in the middle of his empire, a well-oiled machine! The Four of Coins signifies well-managed resources, as this successful gentleman clearly has. On the other hand, it can mean closedness, blocking off and hoarding resources for oneself.

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: A smiling clown holds up a rattlesnake over a hole full of coins in the desert. The clown kneels on her right knee with her right hand on her hip, and she looks up at the yellow snake that she shakes in her left hand. The snake has a long forked tongue, small black fangs, and a rattle on her tail; her eye is open wide. The five coins in the snake's nest are all black, with a yellow outer ring and yellow and orange flowers in the center. The clown has a red painted nose and smile and a yellow wig. She wears a brown cowboy hat with a green five-pointed star in the center and a polka-dotted veil hanging from the brim to protect her neck from the sun. Her button-down shirt is pale blue, and her pants have red and blue checkers. She wears a green bolo tie with a star on it, green gloves, and a green belt. She also has a brown vest decorated with blue five-pointed stars and white horseshoes, with red and white fringe hanging from the bottom. She also wears classic red round-toed clown shoes.
sections.Interpretation: Tempted by gold, a clown dares to stick her hand right into a snake's nest. This card is about materialism and temptation: willingly and imprudently going into danger for the (slim) promise of a reward.

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: With legs crossed, a clown leans back casually against a barrel, spitting three sunflower seeds in an arc in the air. His right elbow rests on the top of the yellow and blue barrel. He holds a cowboy hat in his right hand, which is red and decorated with a purple heart. He cradles a sack of seeds with a picture of a sunflower in his left arm. There is a circle of six coins on the ground in front of him. Each coin is black with an orange edge and a yellow flower encircling a four-petaled orange flower in the center. The ground is sandy and there is a blue fence in the background. The clown's face is painted white, with round red cheeks and blue triangles over his eyes, and he has a round red nose. He is bald on top with curly yellow hair around the sides of his head. His button-down shirt is red with green panels on top, with pictures of red prancing horses and stars. He wears a gold bolo tie and a purple belt with a yellow buckle holds up his pale blue jeans. His cowboy boots are green, with red accents and pictures of gold lassos on the sides.
sections.Interpretation: A rodeo clown takes a break to lay back and spit some seeds. By association with Arcanum VI, The Lover, the Sixes all relate to love, beauty, and other such pleasant things. The Six of Coins is about the simple earthly pleasures.

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: A smiling clown in a dress and bonnet sits in a covered wagon moving through the desert. Six coins float around her head in the wagon. A larger, seventh coin is outside in the sky ahead of her. The clown wears a green dress with a pattern of small orange flowers and a lacy petticoat, with a white bonnet. Her nose and mouth are painted red, and she wears a red wig with braided pigtails. She is surrounded by colorful boxes. The wagon wheels are green with six red spokes and blue hubs. On the edge of the card the blue backs and yellow tails of the two horses drawing the wagon are visible. In the background there are yellow mountains and five green cactuses.
sections.Interpretation: A clown moves across the desert in her covered wagon. All of her earthly possessions are packed up and she is in search of a new place in the world. She is guided to the promise of new wealth, a seventh coin in the sky.

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card:
A clown aims a rifle at an arrangement of targets on a blue curtain. Another clown peeks out from between the curtains in the middle of the targets.

The clown with the rifle is seen from behind, standing in the foreground near some barrels. She wears a wavy red wig and a white cowboy hat with a yellow crescent moon on it. She has a pale blue shirt and a short orange skirt patterned with red and blue flowers and yellow polka dots, and with four red hearts on its green waistband. Her leggings have blue and yellow checkers. She aims the green and yellow rifle with her left hand and has her right hand at the trigger.

Before her, there are eight targets total, arranged in a square in front of blue curtains. Each target is yellow with two red rings encircling a five petaled orange flower with a red center.

The other clown looks more like a circus clown, with a bushy blue wig, red and green face paint, a blue polka dot jumpsuit with a ruffled collar, and red gloves. He pulls the curtains apart with one hand, and looks out in the middle of the square of targets with a neutral expression. A red and white striped banner is tied to the top of the curtains with yellow bows and there are red and green dots of confetti on the sandy ground.
sections.Interpretation: In this card, an Annie-Oakley-type rodeo clown is ready to show off her shooting prowess. But another clown pops up in the middle of her targets – maybe to tease, and maybe to encourage. This card is about concentration, and an opportunity to show off the fruits of your labors.

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: A clown bends down, dropping eight coins in a pile as he tries to gather them. Through the open gate behind him, a bull creeps up. The bull is pale blue with yellow hooves, a red nose, and red curly hair on top of his head. He has a yellow ring through his nose and snorts out plumes of smoke. His golden horns have two red rings around them, and a ninth coin floats between his horns. The bull's eyes are focused on the clown's backside, and the clown seems to be frozen in surprise as he realizes his situation. The clown's face is painted white, with a round red nose and blue cheeks. He wears a curly green wig and a jump suit with stars and stripes like an American flag. His boots are red with gold decorations – the right boot has a picture of fire, and the left boot has a picture of a cactus and the sun. The nine coins are yellow with red five-pointed stars in the center.
sections.Interpretation: The Camoin-Jodorowsky deck which was the main inspiration for Clown Town paints the Nines as crisis cards. The Nine of Coins says that sooner or later, you'll have to lose. In the material sense of Coins, losing can be losing your goods, getting beat up, or both like this clown has in store.

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: Two smiling clowns ride away from the viewer on horseback toward a red setting sun. Ahead of them there are yellow and orange desert mountains with green cactuses sprinkled through them. In the foreground there is a red fence with eight small yellow coins along its top. Each clown carries another yellow coin in a saddle bag. Each coin has a four-petaled flower in its center. The clown on the left side of the card looks back over her shoulder and waves. She wears a dark blue cowboy hat and jeans with a tan shirt, a green bandana, and blue and red boots. Her horse smiles and prances forward. The clown on the right side of the card is turned backward on his horse, who has a more serious expression. He has one hand on his hip and the other hand shielding his eyes. He wears a red cowboy hat, red bandana, and red boots with a green and blue button-down shirt and blue pants.
sections.Interpretation: In this final card of the Suit of Coins, two rodeo clowns go out of the fence and ride off for the sunset. Each clown carries one coin as provisions in her saddlebag. The Camoin-Jodorowsky deck places the four suits in order, transitioning into each other: after Coins comes Batons. Accordingly, these two clowns are ready to go out wandering with the tramp clowns in that suit.

illustrations.note: Hot-linked from the platform mirror (Google Drive); CDN-mirror is a follow-up.

sections.The card: A proud rodeo clown holding up a gold coin. The clown has red, white, and blue face paint and wears a yellow wig with braided pigtails under a light blue cowboy hat. She wears an orange button-down shirt with scalloped collar and cuffs. The front of her shirt is decorated with red and yellow pictures of a horse's head, a bull's head, and six stars. She also wears cowboy boots with stars and stripes in red, blue, and yellow. Her pants are green with patches on the knees and her light-blue gloves match her hat. She holds a large coin up in her right hand. This coin is yellow with a red five-pointed star in the middle. The buckle of her red belt is a smaller but identical coin. Her left hand rests on her hip, holding a looped yellow rope. The ground is sandy and there is a pale blue fence in the background. A yellow horse and bull stand on the other side of the fence.
sections.Interpretation: The Page of Coins stands outside the gates of the rodeo, armed with one coin for spending and another saved on her belt buckle. She hasn't yet faced down the horse and bull who wait beyond, but she is eager to prove herself.

sections.The card: A smiling rodeo clown holding a coin rides on a leaping bull. The clown has orange hair, red cheeks, and a round red nose. He wears a jumpsuit with a pattern of stars and stripes like a big American flag. He also has yellow gloves and yellow, red, and green cowboy boots with pointed toes. His cowboy hat is yellow with eyes and a nose drawn on it. The hat is tied around his neck with a yellow string, and is flying off his head as the bull jumps. In his right hand he holds a rope attached to the bull's horn. In his left hand, he holds up a coin. This coin is yellow with a five-pointed red star in the center, and a four-petaled yellow flower in the center of the star. The bull is pale blue. His nose and hoofs are red. His horns and the curly hair on top of his head are yellow. The ground is sandy and there is a green fence in the background.
sections.Interpretation: The Knight of Coins rides the wild bull, and makes a great show of it too. In the Camoin-Jodorowsky deck, the knights are meant to be the highest rank in the suit, so this guy is the one who has mastered his craft.

sections.The card:
A rodeo clown holding a rifle and a coin.

The clown has red-painted nose, lips, and cheeks, and white eyeshadow. Her wig is yellow and curly, and she also wears a cowboy hat in purple, red, and yellow. She wears a lacy white blouse with an enormous red bow around her collar, and a short red skirt with polka dots in green, blue, and yellow. Her knee-high boots are red with yellow and purple decorations, and she also wears purple gloves with a heart-shaped cutout on the back.

The clown faces the left side of the card and leans back against a light blue bull with yellow horns, hair and hoofs, and a ring through her nose. The clown holds a rifle with a red star on it against her body with her left hand. She holds a coin in her right hand, looking up at it. The coin is yellow with a red five-pointed star in the middle, and a yellow six-petaled flower in the center of the star. The ground is sandy and there is a pale blue fence in the background.
sections.Interpretation: The Queen of Coins is at ease, and her nose-ringed bull seems tamed. She is confident and unafraid.

sections.The card: A rodeo clown sits with legs crossed on a barrel, holding a coin and a flag. The clown has red-painted nose, mouth, and cheeks. His hair is bushy and yellow, sticking out beneath a white cowboy hat decorated with a purple star. He wears blue jeans and a yellow button-down shirt covered in red, orange, green, and blue polka dots. He also wears a red fringed vest decorated with yellow cowboys, a purple belt with a star on its yellow buckle, and white gloves. With his right hand he rests a yellow coin with a red star in the middle against his thigh. His left hand rests on his belt, and a flag flies from a stick that leans against his left shoulder. The flag is red, with a yellow circle in the center containing a green five-pointed star. The ground is sandy and there is a purple fence in the background.
sections.Interpretation: The King of Coins sits with his flag inside the fence, but there is no bull in sight. Perhaps he is staring it down right now.
